feedback.text: Submission: Prohibit killing purchased video games; mandate end-of-life preservation I urge the Commission to ensure the Digital Fairness Act (DFA) stops the practice of rendering lawfully purchased games unplayable when publishers withdraw online services or DRM. This is a widespread harm that current instruments only partly address (e.g., DSA Article 25 on manipulative design; the Digital Content Directive on conformity) and it has now produced concrete consumer disputes (e.g., The Crew became unplayable after a server shutdown). Principle (no-kill): If a game is marketed as a purchase (or licence without a clear end date), continued playability of core gameplay must be guaranteed, even after commercial support ends. Minimum obligations on traders (no opt-out via disclosure): Offline/Server-less fallback. Before withdrawing an essential service or DRM, provideat no extra costan update enabling local play without publisher-controlled servers or keys. Community continuity. If (1) is technically infeasible, provide interoperability information and permission for compatible community-run servers, or release the necessary binaries/keys under a non-commercial licence limited to operability of legitimately acquired copies. (This builds on the EU software interoperability framework.) Escrow & notice. Publish an EOL plan at launch; give 6 months notice before shutdown; escrow decryption keys/documentation with a trusted EU body, automatically released at EOL. Save-data portability. Provide tools to export/import saves and configuration. Currency redemption. Let consumers cash-out unspent in-game currency at face value upon EOL. Contract-override & enforcement. Any ToS term defeating (1)(5) is void; designate violations as black-listed unfair practices with penalties and a private right of action. Why this fits EU law: DSA Art. 25 tackles manipulative interfaces but not post-sale disabling of products; the DFA can close that gap. The Digital Content Directive ensures conformity/updates but does not ensure playability after service withdrawal; DFA can clarify that durability of digital purchases includes operability absent ongoing servers. EU copyright already recognises preservation and interoperability (CDSM Directive Art. 6; Software Directive Art. 6). DFA consumer rules can require traders to make those outcomes feasible in practice (keys, documentation, permissions) and bar contract override. Evidence of harm & public interest: High-profile shutdowns have left paying consumers with nothing; the SKG movement documents consumer detriment and preservation failure. The DFA should codify that publishers may leave the market, but must not destroy access to what consumers already bought.
